1: <header>Anaerobic digestion of poultry litter - A consequential life cycle assessment </header>
Ciara Beausang Kevin McDonnell, Fionnuala Murphy School of Biosystems and Food Engineering, University College Dublin, Belfield, Dublin 4, Ireland
2:
<list>Mono-digestion of poultry litter in lieu of land spreading is assessed. 
Environmental impacts are examined in several scenarios by consequential LCA. 
A potential net reduction in environ- mental impacts is observed for most scenarios. 
Estimated emissions savings of 5723 tonnes CO2 eq annually 
Eutrophication impacts depend on the designated energy pathway.</list>
4: Article history: Received 30 March 2020 Received in revised form 14 May 2020 Accepted 15 May 2020 Available online 17 May 2020
Keywords: LCA Biogas Biomethane Valorisation Bioenergy Circular economy
5: Concentrated land spreading of poultry litter has the potential to cause nutrient build-up in soils, eutrophication of water bodies, air pollution and the spread of pathogens. As a result, alternative routes for the disposal of poul- try litter are being sought. A consequential life cycle assessment was conducted to examine several scenarios where biogas produced from poultry litter is used to generate heat and electricity or is upgraded to biomethane which can substitute natural gas.

For all of the scenarios considered in this study, diverting poultry litter to anaerobic digestion leads to reduced environmental impacts for global warming, fine particulate matter formation and terrestrial acidification. How- ever, the extent to which environmental impacts are reduced varies significantly across the scenarios. Displaced processes including electricity or natural gas, peat moss production and avoided land spreading of litter contrib- ute the most to reducing environmental impacts.

Consequential life cycle assessment is a suitable tool to inform decision-makers about the impact of introducing a new multifunctional technology like anaerobic digestion when considering the systems which are displaced. The results show the range of potential environmental outcomes, rather than predicting a single most-likely outcome. The results of this study indicate that anaerobic digestion is a suitable disposal route for poultry litter which may lead to reduced environmental impacts. This type of analysis is recommended when considering alternative feed- stocks and valorisation pathways in the circular economy.

7: Several challenges to global environmental, social and economic sustainability are driving the development of the circular economy,

the aim of which is to maintain the value of products, materials and resources for as long as possible and to minimise the generation of waste (European Commission, 2015). These challenges include elim- inating the use of fossil fuels, mitigating climate change and ensuring global food security. The bioeconomy is often represented as the bi- ological element of the circular economy, which focuses on the utilisation of biomass in primary production processes (forestry, fisheries and agriculture) and increased valorisation of raw materials used (International Energy Agency, 2018). Anaerobic digestion (AD) is gaining attention as a potential technology to mitigate greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions and to convert low-value material to high- value material in the bioeconomy (International Energy Agency, 2018). In AD systems, organic matter is converted to biogas in the ab- sence of oxygen (Scarlat et al., 2018). Biogas consists of methane (CH4), carbon dioxide (CO2) and other trace gases (Hakawati et al., 2017). There are various utilisation pathways for both raw and upgraded forms of biogas in electricity, heat and transport (Hakawati et al., 2017).

Biogas can be derived from a wide range of feedstocks, including ag- ricultural residues (livestock manure, crop residues), energy crops, in- dustrial residues from the food and beverage industry, biowaste, municipal organic waste and sewage sludge (Scarlat et al., 2018). Due to concerns regarding the sustainability of energy crops and their im- pact on land use change and food security, several European countries are limiting the share of energy crops used for biogas production 1,,2018). The use of energy crops in future biogas production in Europe is expected to be increasingly limited and support will instead be directed to the use of waste and residues such as manure and food waste (Scarlat et al., 2018).

Poultry litter has significant potential as a feedstock for AD. Poul- try production has been increasing globally, with an annual average growth rate of 5% over the last 50 years (Mottet and Tempio, 2017). One of the major challenges for the poultry industry is the accumula- tion of wastes generated by intensive production, especially litter (Bolan al., 2010). Traditionally poultry litter is land spread as a soil amendment, however, concentrated spreading can lead to nutri- ent build-up in soils (Chaump et al., 2019). This in turn can contrib- ute to eutrophication of water bodies, air pollution and the spread of pathogens. As a result, there is an interest in alternative disposal routes for poultry litter. While AD has been used extensively to treat other types of manures, such as dairy and swine, it remains underutilised in the poultry industry. Poultry litter can be a challeng- ing feedstock for AD, particularly due to high concentrations of am- monium (Hassanein et al., 2019). However, recent advances including multi-stage digestion, the reduction of ammonium con- centrations, thermochemical pre-treatment and bioaugmentation indicate that AD has the potential to stabilise poultry litter and trans- form it into useful products (Hassanein et al., 2019).

Life cycle assessment (LCA) is a suitable tool to assess the envi- ronmental sustainability of AD systems (Czyrnek-Delêtre et al., 2017). LCA is the compilation and evaluation of the inputs, outputs and potential environmental impacts of a product system through- out its life cycle There are two types of LCA: attributional LCA (ALCA) and consequential LCA (CLCA). ALCA follows an account- ing perspective, which tracks energy and material flows along a product's supply chain, during use and disposal or recycling (Plevin et al., 2014). CLCA is a modelling approach where "activities are in- cluded in the product system to the extent that they are expected to change as a consequence of change in demand for the functional unit" (UNEP/SETAC, 2011). When biological resources are diverted to new valorisation pathways like AD, it is important to consider the potential consequences introducing a new technology may have, intended or otherwise. The International Reference Life Cycle Data System (ILCD) handbook recommends CLCA for analyses that will inform policy making and ALCA only in contexts where no deci- sion is to be made based on the results of the analysis (EC-JRC,2010)

Furthermore, as stakeholders must take responsibility for the activi- ties in the consequential product life cycle (Weidema et al., 2018), CLCA is deemed to be the most relevant modelling approach in this context.

CLCAs are typically linear models which do not consider market characteristics such as substitution, price effects, elasticity of demand and supply, and rebound effects (Yang and Heijungs, 2018). Recent CLCAs have incorporated models from different fields to compensate for this, including partial equilibrium (PE) and computable general equilibrium (CGE) models, which are more mathematically sophisti- cated in representing market mechanisms (Yang and Heijungs, 2018). However, the limitations of PE and CGE have also been recognised, largely due to their underlying theoretical assumptions, which may not be observed in real markets (Yang and Heijungs, 2018, Kätelhön et al., 2016). The Technology Choice Model (TCM) has been proposed as an alternative model to integrate market mechanisms in CLCA (Kätelhön et al., 2016) and has been applied to examine the increase in demand for a food product in an agricultural system where constraint of production is predicted (Larrea-Gallegos et al., 2018). However, the data required for TCM can be higher than for PE and CGE. Due to the strengths and limitations of different classes of models, Yang and Heijungs, 2018 recommend that the results of CLCAs should be evalu- ated collectively alongside nonlinear models, rather than combining the two. Furthermore, the importance of relaxing assumptions common to CLCAs, such as 1:1 substitution, has been emphasised (Yang and Heijungs, 2018).

CLCA has been widely used to assess AD of feedstocks including pig and cow manure, food waste, maize and grass silage (Tsapekos et al., 2019; Pehme et al., 2017; Styles et al., 2016; Van Stappen et al., 2016; Vázquez-Rowe et al., 2014; De Vries et al., 2012; Hamelin et al., 2011). Previous CLCAs have shown that diverting feedstocks to AD may have potential unintended consequences. For example, Van Stappen et al. (2016) examined the impacts of a farm scale biogas plant and found that feedstock that is used for animal feed should not be used as feed- stock for AD due to additional impacts mainly in the form of extra agri- cultural land required for replacement animal feed. Similarly Styles et al. (2016) recommended that digestion of food waste and manures should be incentivised to maximise GHG abatement and to avoid carbon leak- age associated with the use of crops and wastes otherwise used to feed livestock. Pehme et al. (2017) found that co-digestion of manure with cultivated grass led to higher environmental impacts due to addi- tional demands for land and fertilisers compared with unused grass from semi-natural grasslands.

As valorisation pathways continue to be developed in the circular economy, a clear understanding of their environmental impact is re- quired. This will allow the best use for a particular feedstock to be iden- tified. Poultry litter can alternatively be used as a soil amendment through the production of biochar (Sikder and Joardar, 2019) or for en- ergy recovery through thermal treatment such as combustion, pyrolysis and gasification (Jeswani et al., 2019). While ALCA has been used to as- sess the sustainability of poultry litter gasification for electricity and heat generation (Jeswani et al., 2019), the valorisation of poultry litter has not yet been examined using CLCA. This study assesses the environ- mental consequences of increased biogas production from poultry litter. Scenario analysis is used to investigate common assumptions regarding market mechanisms in CLCA which have been identified in the literature.

As valorisation pathways continue to be developed in the circular economy, a clear understanding of their environmental impact is re- quired. This will allow the best use for a particular feedstock to be iden- tified. Poultry litter can alternatively be used as a soil amendment through the production of biochar (Sikder and Joardar, 2019) or for en- ergy recovery through thermal treatment such as combustion, pyrolysis and gasification (Jeswani et al., 2019). While ALCA has been used to as- sess the sustainability of poultry litter gasification for electricity and heat generation (Jeswani et al., 2019), the valorisation of poultry litter has not yet been examined using CLCA. This study assesses the environ- mental consequences of increased biogas production from poultry litter. Scenario analysis is used to investigate common assumptions regarding market mechanisms in CLCA which have been identified in the literature.
11: Several challenges to global environmental, social and economic sustainability are driving the development of the circular economy,

the aim of which is to maintain the value of products, materials and resources for as long as possible and to minimise the generation of waste (European Commission, 2015). These challenges include elim- inating the use of fossil fuels, mitigating climate change and ensuring global food security. The bioeconomy is often represented as the bi- ological element of the circular economy, which focuses on the utilisation of biomass in primary production processes (forestry, fisheries and agriculture) and increased valorisation of raw materials used (International Energy Agency, 2018). Anaerobic digestion (AD) is gaining attention as a potential technology to mitigate greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions and to convert low-value material to high- value material in the bioeconomy (International Energy Agency, 2018). In AD systems, organic matter is converted to biogas in the ab- sence of oxygen (Scarlat et al., 2018). Biogas consists of methane (CH4), carbon dioxide (CO2) and other trace gases (Hakawati et al., 2017). There are various utilisation pathways for both raw and upgraded forms of biogas in electricity, heat and transport (Hakawati et al., 2017).

Biogas can be derived from a wide range of feedstocks, including ag- ricultural residues (livestock manure, crop residues), energy crops, in- dustrial residues from the food and beverage industry, biowaste, municipal organic waste and sewage sludge (Scarlat et al., 2018). Due to concerns regarding the sustainability of energy crops and their im- pact on land use change and food security, several European countries are limiting the share of energy crops used for biogas production 1,,2018). The use of energy crops in future biogas production in Europe is expected to be increasingly limited and support will instead be directed to the use of waste and residues such as manure and food waste (Scarlat et al., 2018).

Poultry litter has significant potential as a feedstock for AD. Poul- try production has been increasing globally, with an annual average growth rate of 5% over the last 50 years (Mottet and Tempio, 2017). One of the major challenges for the poultry industry is the accumula- tion of wastes generated by intensive production, especially litter (Bolan al., 2010). Traditionally poultry litter is land spread as a soil amendment, however, concentrated spreading can lead to nutri- ent build-up in soils (Chaump et al., 2019). This in turn can contrib- ute to eutrophication of water bodies, air pollution and the spread of pathogens. As a result, there is an interest in alternative disposal routes for poultry litter. While AD has been used extensively to treat other types of manures, such as dairy and swine, it remains underutilised in the poultry industry. Poultry litter can be a challeng- ing feedstock for AD, particularly due to high concentrations of am- monium (Hassanein et al., 2019). However, recent advances including multi-stage digestion, the reduction of ammonium con- centrations, thermochemical pre-treatment and bioaugmentation indicate that AD has the potential to stabilise poultry litter and trans- form it into useful products (Hassanein et al., 2019).

Life cycle assessment (LCA) is a suitable tool to assess the envi- ronmental sustainability of AD systems (Czyrnek-Delêtre et al., 2017). LCA is the compilation and evaluation of the inputs, outputs and potential environmental impacts of a product system through- out its life cycle There are two types of LCA: attributional LCA (ALCA) and consequential LCA (CLCA). ALCA follows an account- ing perspective, which tracks energy and material flows along a product's supply chain, during use and disposal or recycling (Plevin et al., 2014). CLCA is a modelling approach where "activities are in- cluded in the product system to the extent that they are expected to change as a consequence of change in demand for the functional unit" (UNEP/SETAC, 2011). When biological resources are diverted to new valorisation pathways like AD, it is important to consider the potential consequences introducing a new technology may have, intended or otherwise. The International Reference Life Cycle Data System (ILCD) handbook recommends CLCA for analyses that will inform policy making and ALCA only in contexts where no deci- sion is to be made based on the results of the analysis (EC-JRC,2010)

Furthermore, as stakeholders must take responsibility for the activi- ties in the consequential product life cycle (Weidema et al., 2018), CLCA is deemed to be the most relevant modelling approach in this context.

CLCAs are typically linear models which do not consider market characteristics such as substitution, price effects, elasticity of demand and supply, and rebound effects (Yang and Heijungs, 2018). Recent CLCAs have incorporated models from different fields to compensate for this, including partial equilibrium (PE) and computable general equilibrium (CGE) models, which are more mathematically sophisti- cated in representing market mechanisms (Yang and Heijungs, 2018). However, the limitations of PE and CGE have also been recognised, largely due to their underlying theoretical assumptions, which may not be observed in real markets (Yang and Heijungs, 2018, Kätelhön et al., 2016). The Technology Choice Model (TCM) has been proposed as an alternative model to integrate market mechanisms in CLCA (Kätelhön et al., 2016) and has been applied to examine the increase in demand for a food product in an agricultural system where constraint of production is predicted (Larrea-Gallegos et al., 2018). However, the data required for TCM can be higher than for PE and CGE. Due to the strengths and limitations of different classes of models, Yang and Heijungs, 2018 recommend that the results of CLCAs should be evalu- ated collectively alongside nonlinear models, rather than combining the two. Furthermore, the importance of relaxing assumptions common to CLCAs, such as 1:1 substitution, has been emphasised (Yang and Heijungs, 2018).

CLCA has been widely used to assess AD of feedstocks including pig and cow manure, food waste, maize and grass silage (Tsapekos et al., 2019; Pehme et al., 2017; Styles et al., 2016; Van Stappen et al., 2016; Vázquez-Rowe et al., 2014; De Vries et al., 2012; Hamelin et al., 2011). Previous CLCAs have shown that diverting feedstocks to AD may have potential unintended consequences. For example, Van Stappen et al. (2016) examined the impacts of a farm scale biogas plant and found that feedstock that is used for animal feed should not be used as feed- stock for AD due to additional impacts mainly in the form of extra agri- cultural land required for replacement animal feed. Similarly Styles et al. (2016) recommended that digestion of food waste and manures should be incentivised to maximise GHG abatement and to avoid carbon leak- age associated with the use of crops and wastes otherwise used to feed livestock. Pehme et al. (2017) found that co-digestion of manure with cultivated grass led to higher environmental impacts due to addi- tional demands for land and fertilisers compared with unused grass from semi-natural grasslands.

As valorisation pathways continue to be developed in the circular economy, a clear understanding of their environmental impact is re- quired. This will allow the best use for a particular feedstock to be iden- tified. Poultry litter can alternatively be used as a soil amendment through the production of biochar (Sikder and Joardar, 2019) or for en- ergy recovery through thermal treatment such as combustion, pyrolysis and gasification (Jeswani et al., 2019). While ALCA has been used to as- sess the sustainability of poultry litter gasification for electricity and heat generation (Jeswani et al., 2019), the valorisation of poultry litter has not yet been examined using CLCA. This study assesses the environ- mental consequences of increased biogas production from poultry litter. Scenario analysis is used to investigate common assumptions regarding market mechanisms in CLCA which have been identified in the literature.

15: The methodology for this CLCA will now be described as follows; feedstock availability (Section 2.1), goal, scope and boundary definition (Section 2.2), life cycle inventory (Section 2.3), life cycle impact assess- ment (Section scenario analysis (Section 2.5) and uncertainty anal- ysis (Section 2.6).
16: Poultry litter is a mix of bedding material (mainly wood shavings, straw or paper), feathers, manure, urine and food particles left behind when chickens are sent for slaughter (Sustainable Energy Ireland, 2003). In Ireland poultry litter is disposed of by land spreading or is used in the production of mushroom compost (Sustainable Energy Ireland, 2003). While poultry litter is a valuable soil fertiliser, it poses a risk of transmitting botulism to cattle if control measures are not followed (DAFM, 2019). As a result, there is a need to consider alterna- tive options for disposal.

The geographical scope of this study is County Monaghan, Ireland (Fig. 1), where poultry production is particularly concentrated (Central Statistics Office, 2018). To determine the potential poultry litter resource in Monaghan, Environmental Impact Statements (EIS) for poultry production facilities undergoing application for a licence were sourced from the Irish Environmental Protection Agency (EPA). An In- dustrial Emissions (IE) licence is required for the rearing of poultry in in- stallations where the capacity exceeds 40,000 bird places (Environmental Protection Agency, 2020). In this assessment, only litter produced from broilers is included; litter from layers and pullets is ex- cluded as the majority of poultry numbers in the region is made up of broiler chickens. A search on the EPA website for IE licence applications returned 24 results for facilities undergoing application for a licence as of the 15th of October 2019. Half of these are for new facilities, while the other half are existing facilities seeking to expand production. The

quantity of litter was estimated using the number of additional bird places from facilities applying for a licence (2,368,100). The amount of litter generated annually by broilers is 1.2 tonnes per 1000 birds (Lichtenberg et al., 2002). Assuming 7 batches per year, this gives 20,000 tonnes of litter annually in addition to what is currently pro- duced. In this study it is assumed that the additional litter from facilities undergoing application for a licence would be in excess of the demand for litter used in mushroom compost production and would otherwise be disposed of by land spreading.
17: Poultry litter is a mix of bedding material (mainly wood shavings, straw or paper), feathers, manure, urine and food particles left behind when chickens are sent for slaughter (Sustainable Energy Ireland, 2003). In Ireland poultry litter is disposed of by land spreading or is used in the production of mushroom compost (Sustainable Energy Ireland, 2003). While poultry litter is a valuable soil fertiliser, it poses a risk of transmitting botulism to cattle if control measures are not followed (DAFM, 2019). As a result, there is a need to consider alterna- tive options for disposal.

The geographical scope of this study is County Monaghan, Ireland (Fig. 1), where poultry production is particularly concentrated (Central Statistics Office, 2018). To determine the potential poultry litter resource in Monaghan, Environmental Impact Statements (EIS) for poultry production facilities undergoing application for a licence were sourced from the Irish Environmental Protection Agency (EPA). An In- dustrial Emissions (IE) licence is required for the rearing of poultry in in- stallations where the capacity exceeds 40,000 bird places (Environmental Protection Agency, 2020). In this assessment, only litter produced from broilers is included; litter from layers and pullets is ex- cluded as the majority of poultry numbers in the region is made up of broiler chickens. A search on the EPA website for IE licence applications returned 24 results for facilities undergoing application for a licence as of the 15th of October 2019. Half of these are for new facilities, while the other half are existing facilities seeking to expand production. The

quantity of litter was estimated using the number of additional bird places from facilities applying for a licence (2,368,100). The amount of litter generated annually by broilers is 1.2 tonnes per 1000 birds (Lichtenberg et al., 2002). Assuming 7 batches per year, this gives 20,000 tonnes of litter annually in addition to what is currently pro- duced. In this study it is assumed that the additional litter from facilities undergoing application for a licence would be in excess of the demand for litter used in mushroom compost production and would otherwise be disposed of by land spreading.

19: The goal of the study is to assess the environmental impact of divert- ing poultry litter from its current use of land spreading to AD via the in- stallation of a hypothetical biogas plant. We are interested in the effects of utilising the excess resource of poultry litter identified in the region specified in this study. Based on the theoretical potential of poultry litter determined in Section 2.1, the functional unit is based on the amount of litter available for digestion annually and is defined as "one year of bio- gas plant operation". The reference flow is 20,000 tonnes of litter. The intended application of this study is to provide an evidence-based re- source for policymakers and researchers which can be used in combina- tion with other complimentary models and analyses. The system boundaries were defined according to the guidelines provided in the ILCD handbook (2010), along with data available from previous CLCA studies (Styles et al., 2016; Van Stappen et al., 2016).
Fig. 1. Geographic scope of the study: Co. Monaghan, Ireland.
Fig. 2. System boundary for the major processes considered in this study. Incurred processes are shown in boxes with a solid outline and avoided processes are show in boxes with a dotted outline.

The system boundaries are shown in Fig. 2 and include operation of the biogas plant (transport of litter, digester and CHP operation, digestate storage and use), the processes displaced by the two co-products (bio- gas and digestate) and the processes displaced by the initial use of the poultry litter (land spreading). Based on the framework proposed by Ekvall and Weidema (2004) for multifunctional processes, broiler pro- duction is excluded from the system boundaries. The production vol- ume of poultry litter depends on the demand for broilers. It is assumed that changing the use of poultry litter in the system being in- vestigated will not affect broiler production, which depends on the de- mand for broilers only. Therefore, broiler production is excluded (Ekvall and Weidema, 2004). As the geographical scope of this study is County Monaghan, Ireland, foreground data is based on an Irish context. Back- ground data is from the Ecoinvent database version 3.5 consequential system model (Wernet et al., 2016). The specific datasets are listed in the Supplementary information. The data for the life cycle inventory has been made available in the repository Mendeley Data (see "Data availability").

22: The AD system in this study is based on operational data from an existing facility in Ireland. The plant parameters were modified for the reference flow of 20,000 tonnes of poultry litter being considered in this study based on the examples provided by Gill and Fleming (2009). Table 1 lists the plant parameters proposed for this study. The proposed plant is an independent entity which is fed with poul- try litter from contracted farms in the region. Digestion takes place in a two-stage process. The digesters operate at mesophilic tempera- tures of 37 °C and the total digestion time is 34 days. The biogas con- sists of 56% methane. Fugitive emissions are assumed to be 2.4% of methane produced (Scheutz and Fredenslund, 2019). Following di- gestion, the digestate is pasteurised at 70 °C for one hour to satisfy Animal By-Product Regulations (European Union, 2014). The biogas produced is used for combined heat and power production (CHP). The biogas engine has 42% efficiency for heat and 42% efficiency for electricity (Table 1). All of the heat produced is used in the digestion and pasteurisation processes. The electricity generated is exported to the grid.

24: CLCA uses market information to identify which activities are af- fected by a change in demand for the functional unit (Pehme et al., 2017). In this study each co-product (biogas and digestate) is assumed to substitute short term marginal technologies. These are existing tech- nologies whose output changes due to small changes in demand in the market (Van Stappen et al., 2016). If the affected process is a specific technology, this is the marginal technology. If the market trend is in- creasing, the marginal technology will be found amongst the most com- petitive suppliers, or amongst the least competitive if the market trend is decreasing (Lund et al., 2010). Constrained technologies are excluded. These are technologies which are limited in their ability to change pro- duction volume in response to a change in demand for product output. The electricity produced by the biogas plant is supplied to the grid, which displaces electricity generated by other fuels. In CLCAs for biogas, frequently a single marginal technology is identified, for example elec- tricity generation from natural gas or coal (Pehme et al., 2017; Styles et al., 2016). However, Lund et al. (2010) argue that a marginal change in capacity will have to operate as an integrated part of the total energy system and the marginal source of electricity will be a mixture of differ- ent technologies, using different fuels. Mathiesen et al. (2009) suggest

Midpoint impact category|Characterisation factor|Indicator unit Climate change Fine particulate matter formation|Global warming potential (GWP) Particulate matter formation potential (PMFP)|kg CO2 eq. to air kg PM2.5 eq. to air Terrestrial acidification|Terrestrial acidification potential (TAP)|kg SO2 eq. to air Freshwater eutrophication|Freshwater eutrophication potential (FEP)|kg P eq. to freshwater

that LCA should include different technologies, including those unable to adjust to changes in demand, such as wind. Furthermore, they sug- gest using long-term perspectives by identifying affected technologies in several different future scenarios, i.e. both fossil and renewable en- ergy technologies, through a sensitivity analysis.

In this study, the electricity mix that is displaced is based on Ireland's Draft National Energy and Climate Plan (NECP) (Government of Ireland, 2018a). The mix is based on the NECP 2 scenario which assumes high oil prices and includes additional measures. The mix for the selected year (2024) consists of the following fuels for electricity generation; 45% nat- ural gas, 22% coal, 2% peat, 23% wind and 8% biomass. As this CLCA is for a proposed plant using current estimates of litter availability, the year 2024 was chosen in order to allow planning and construction timelines to be considered, as well as improved renewable penetration in the short term. The impact of the displaced electricity mix is considered in a sensitivity analysis.

Digestate from the plant is separated into solid and liquid fractions. The solid fraction is used in the horticulture sector where it displaces peat. Ireland has a long history of peat extraction and peat is the main constituent in growing media in Ireland (Schmilewski, 2017). The am- monia stripper that is used generates an ammonia sulphate solution from the liquid fraction which can be used to replace artificial nitrogen fertiliser. However, the poultry litter previously used as an organic fertiliser and now fed to the digester needs to be replaced by mineral fertiliser. According to the Teagasc Fertiliser Use Survey 2005-2015 (Dillon et al., 2018), Calcium Ammonium Nitrate (CAN) is the most commonly used fertiliser in Ireland for N nutrition.

4: Avoided field emissions from the displaced poultry litter are in- cluded in the analysis, as well as the emissions incurred from the addi- tional CAN that replaces poultry litter. The fertiliser replacement value of the poultry litter is determined using the MANNER-NPK software v1.01 (ADAS, 2013), assuming February application to a spring crop on sandy loam soils, with broadcast spreading as the selected applica- tion method. Nitrous oxide (N20) emissions are based on the Intergovernmnetal Panel on Climate Change, (2006) methodology. The tier 1 approach is used for poultry litter, while a tier 2 approach is used for CAN as per Ireland's National Inventory (Environmental Protection Agency, 2019). Ammonia (NH3) emissions are calculated using the European Environmental Agency (2019) methodology; the tier 1 approach is used for poultry litter, while a tier 2 approach is used for CAN. The digestate is stored in a closed tank. Methane emis- sions from digestate storage are included in the overall estimate for fu- gitive emissions at 2.4% (Section 2.3.1). N20 and NH3 emissions from digestate storage are calculated as per Styles et al. (2016). Emissions from the CHP plant are estimated using the emission factors for Danish decentralised CHP plants by Nielsen et al. (2010), with a value of 1.6 N20 and 202 nitrogen oxides (NOx) per GJ of biogas combusted.
5: The impact from the construction of the AD plant and CHP unit are included in this study. The data for the AD plant and CHP unit are

from the Ecoinvent database version 3.5 cut-off system model. As the datasets correspond to smaller plant sizes (500 m³ AD and 160 kWe CHP), the environmental impacts are estimated by scaling up capacity to match the sizes considered in this study (5000 m³ AD and 1500 kWe CHP). This is based on the approach for cost estimation in scaling up process plants (Coulson et al., 1993) and with regard to scal- ing for estimating environmental impacts as per Whiting and Azapagic (2014):

where E2 = environmental impacts of the larger plant (AD or CHP); E1 = environmental impacts of the smaller plant (AD or CHP); C2 = ca- pacity of the larger plant (volume for the AD plant and installed power for the CHP plant); C1 = capacity of the smaller plant (volume for the AD plant and installed power for the CHP unit); 0.6 : scaling factor.
6: The software used in this assessment is openLCA v1.9.0 (GreenDelta, 2019). ReCiPe 2016 v.1.1 (Huijbregts et al., 2017) is the selected LCIA method as it is the most comprehensive and recently updated impact assessment method available in openLCA v1.9.0. The selected character- isation factors are at the midpoint level, as they have a stronger relation to the environmental flows and a relatively low uncertainty (Huijbregts et al., 2017). ReCiPe 2016 has three perspectives in order to group value choices in the derivation of characterisation factors: individualist, hierarchist and egalitarian. In this study, the hierarchist perspective is used as it considers climate change on a 100-year time horizon (i.e. GWP100) and includes climate-carbon feedbacks as per the Fifth As- sessment Report of the IPCC (2014).

The selected impact categories, characterisation factors and indica- tor units are listed in Table 2. Climate change, acidification and eutro- phication are recommended impact categories for LCA of bioenergy systems (Czyrnek-Delêtre et al., 2017). Furthermore, these impact cate- gories relate to carbon, nitrogen and phosphorous flows that are rele- vant for agricultural systems (Pehme et al., 2017). With regards to particulate matter (PM), biogas combustion is not a significant emission source when compared to other fuels, however, secondary PM forma- tion can occur due to NOx emissions from CHP and NH3 volatilisation from storage and digestate final use (Paolini et al., 2018). The impact factor values for the selected impact categories are listed in the Supple- mentary information.

8: From the baseline scenario, key parameters were identified and tested in a scenario analysis to determine their contribution to the over- all results. These will now be described.
9: To construct a practical model for CLCA it is often necessary to sim- plify the system without losing too much relevant information. One simplification is the ceteris paribus assumption: that the demand for the functions fulfilled by other product life cycles is constant (Ekvall, 2000). In other words, the convention is to assume a 1:1 substitution ratio between functionally equivalent product systems and to assume no indirect or scale effects in any system anywhere in the world, at any time (Chalmers et al., 2015; Plevin et al., 2014). In the case of fossil fuels, it is assumed that not only is the consumption of the fuel avoided, but the corresponding quantity of fuel is assumed not to be produced, transported, or refined (Plevin et al., 2014).

In reality there is no perfect substitution and there are indirect and scale effects. The ceteris paribus assumption is a significant source of un- certainty in CLCA. Research by York (2012) found that each unit of total

national energy use from renewable sources displaced less than one- quarter of a unit of fossil-fuel energy use. In the United States, de Gorter and Drabik (2011) found that one energy-equivalent gallon of corn ethanol replaces only 0.35 to 0.50 gallons of gasoline. If price elas- ticity of supply and demand for the products of interest is known, the actual substitution ratio can be determined. However, in practice it may be difficult to obtain sufficient estimates for the elasticities of sup- ply and demand.

Ekvall (2000) examined the issue of substitution in LCAs of recycled materials. In many LCA studies, recycled material is assumed to replace virgin material, when in fact it may replace recycled material from other sources, different types of material or no material at all. Ekvall uses re- ported price elasticities to calculate the substitution ratio for recycled materials. In the case that price elasticities are not available, Ekvall sug- gests using a "50/50 assumption" as a crude approximation of indirect effects, in which it is assumed that 50% of the recycled material replaces virgin material and the remaining 50% replaces recycled material from other sources. Ekvall recommends using sensitivity analysis to consider the large uncertainty in this assumption, for example where recycled material is assumed to replace, e.g. virgin material only.

The substitution ratio for the two co-products is considered in Sce- narios 1, 6 and 7 of the sensitivity analysis. The baseline model assumes a 1:1 substitution ratio for digestate fibre and peat moss based on mass. In Scenario 1, a substitution ratio of 1:0.5 is used for digestate fibre to peat moss. In Scenarios 6 and 7, biogas is upgraded to biomethane which is assumed to substitute natural gas. The ratios used are 1:0.5 and 1:0.2 for Scenarios 6 and 7 respectively. These ratios are based on the upper estimates by de Gorter and Drabik (2011) and York (2012).
10: To construct a practical model for CLCA it is often necessary to sim- plify the system without losing too much relevant information. One simplification is the ceteris paribus assumption: that the demand for the functions fulfilled by other product life cycles is constant (Ekvall, 2000). In other words, the convention is to assume a 1:1 substitution ratio between functionally equivalent product systems and to assume no indirect or scale effects in any system anywhere in the world, at any time (Chalmers et al., 2015; Plevin et al., 2014). In the case of fossil fuels, it is assumed that not only is the consumption of the fuel avoided, but the corresponding quantity of fuel is assumed not to be produced, transported, or refined (Plevin et al., 2014).

In reality there is no perfect substitution and there are indirect and scale effects. The ceteris paribus assumption is a significant source of un- certainty in CLCA. Research by York (2012) found that each unit of total

national energy use from renewable sources displaced less than one- quarter of a unit of fossil-fuel energy use. In the United States, de Gorter and Drabik (2011) found that one energy-equivalent gallon of corn ethanol replaces only 0.35 to 0.50 gallons of gasoline. If price elas- ticity of supply and demand for the products of interest is known, the actual substitution ratio can be determined. However, in practice it may be difficult to obtain sufficient estimates for the elasticities of sup- ply and demand.

Ekvall (2000) examined the issue of substitution in LCAs of recycled materials. In many LCA studies, recycled material is assumed to replace virgin material, when in fact it may replace recycled material from other sources, different types of material or no material at all. Ekvall uses re- ported price elasticities to calculate the substitution ratio for recycled materials. In the case that price elasticities are not available, Ekvall sug- gests using a "50/50 assumption" as a crude approximation of indirect effects, in which it is assumed that 50% of the recycled material replaces virgin material and the remaining 50% replaces recycled material from other sources. Ekvall recommends using sensitivity analysis to consider the large uncertainty in this assumption, for example where recycled material is assumed to replace, e.g. virgin material only.

The substitution ratio for the two co-products is considered in Sce- narios 1, 6 and 7 of the sensitivity analysis. The baseline model assumes a 1:1 substitution ratio for digestate fibre and peat moss based on mass. In Scenario 1, a substitution ratio of 1:0.5 is used for digestate fibre to peat moss. In Scenarios 6 and 7, biogas is upgraded to biomethane which is assumed to substitute natural gas. The ratios used are 1:0.5 and 1:0.2 for Scenarios 6 and 7 respectively. These ratios are based on the upper estimates by de Gorter and Drabik (2011) and York (2012).

The substitution ratio for the two co-products is considered in Sce- narios 1, 6 and 7 of the sensitivity analysis. The baseline model assumes a 1:1 substitution ratio for digestate fibre and peat moss based on mass. In Scenario 1, a substitution ratio of 1:0.5 is used for digestate fibre to peat moss. These ratios are based on the upper estimates by de Gorter and Drabik (2011) and York (2012).
13: In the baseline model, it is assumed that digestate fibre displaces peat moss. Ireland has been a significant producer of growing media in the EU through the extraction of peat and peat moss is currently the most prevalent growing medium used in Ireland (Schmilewski, 2017). However, the extraction of peat has negative environmental im- pacts. In 2019, Bord na Móna, the largest peat extraction company in Ireland, announced plans to reduce horticultural peat sales by 50% and to transition to non-peat based horticultural products (JOCCA, 2019). Therefore it is likely that peat moss production in Ireland will become constrained in the near future. According to Schmilewski (2017), the next most prevalent growing medium in Ireland is coir (coconut fibre). In Scenario 2, digestate is assumed to displace coir instead of peat moss.
14: Many LCA studies exclude capital goods that are necessary to manu- facture a good or to provide a service (Frischknecht et al., 2007). This may be due to a lack of data (Pehme et al., 2017) or due to guidelines which suggest that the inclusion of capital goods is not required (Styles et al., 2015). Capital goods are difficult to model and are there- fore commonly based on rough estimates, even in international data- bases (Silva et al., 2018). However, in the ISO standards capital goods are explicitly part of the product system, thus it is doubtful if they can be excluded per se (Frischknecht et al., 2007). In the baseline model the unit processes for AD and CHP plant construction which are based on Ecoinvent datasets (Wernet et al., 2016) are included in the inven- tory. In Scenario 3, these processes are excluded to examine their contri- bution to the overall results.
15: The fuel mix for displaced electricity generation in the baseline model is based on the NECP 2 scenario for the year 2024. There is a sig- nificant amount of coal (22%) in this fuel mix for the chosen year. As Ireland increases its climate ambition, a higher proportion of electricity generation will come from renewable energy. Ireland's National Devel- opment Plan (2018-2027) included the commitment to end the

burning of coal for Irish power generation by 2025 (Government of Ireland, 2018b). In Scenario 4, the fuel mix for displaced electricity gen- eration is based on the NECP 2 scenario for the year 2030, which ex- cludes coal and has a higher proportion of renewables (Table 3).
16: The predominant use for biogas in Ireland has been electricity and heat production via CHPs. However, there is increasing interest in upgrading biogas to biomethane for injection to the gas grid or for use as a transport fuel. According to Gas Networks Ireland, biomethane may constitute 18% of the gas supply by 2030 (Gas Networks Ireland, 2019). In Scenario 5, it is assumed that 90% of the biogas produced is upgraded to biomethane for injection to the gas grid, while the remain- ing 10% is combusted onsite in a CHP to provide heat and power for the biogas plant. The upgrading technology is assumed to be water scrub- bing using the burdens of 770 MJ of electricity and 129 kg of water per tonne of CO2 removal, as per Tsapekos et al. (2019). The avoided burdens from natural gas heating were taken from the Ecoinvent data- base version 3.5 consequential system model (see Supplementary information).
17: In the baseline model, fugitive emissions are taken as 2.4% of biomethane produced (Section 2.3.1). Estimates of fugitive emissions are limited by the duration of measurement, which may be as short as 1 day of operation in a given year. The most important sources of fugi- tive emissions include open storage of the digestate, the CHP engine, leaks and the pressure release valve (International Energy Agency, 2017). In some studies, fugitive emissions have been reported to be as high as 4% (Fredenslund et al., 2018; Groth et al., 2015). In scenario 8, fu- gitive emissions are taken as 4% of biomethane produced.
18: The uncertainty associated with the results depends on the uncer- tainty of the model parameters. Uncertainty for the scenario and sensi- tivity analysis was quantified by applying error propagation via Monte Carlo simulation (MCS) implemented with openLCA 1.9.0. Data quality indicators in pedigree matrices were used to derive standard deviations which were attached to each parameter in the process inventories. The pedigree matrices allow data reliability, completeness and temporal, geographical and technological correlations to be considered (Ciroth et al., 2013). Characteristic results were calculated for each scenario with 1000 iterations with dependent sampling (Henriksson et al., 2014). The data quality indicators are included in the life cycle inventory dataset which is linked to this article (see "Data availability").

22: For the global warming impact category, the results suggest a poten- tial net reduction in emissions in the baseline scenario by implementing AD of poultry litter compared with land spreading. The processes that are displaced in the system, namely avoided electricity generation and avoided peat moss production, have the largest negative values which contribute to the overall result. The processes with the highest positive values for GWP include incurred fertiliser production and fugitive emis- sions from the plant. Incurred field emissions arising from nitrogen fertiliser in lieu of poultry litter along with transport are also significant sources of emissions in the product system. In the particulate matter impact category, there is a net reduction in the baseline scenario. The emissions avoided from land spreading of lit- ter are the most significant process which results in a negative value. Avoided electricity production is also significant, largely due to the avoided coal in the fuel mix. The processes with the largest positive values in terms of PM2.5 eq emissions are the production of the ammo- nia stripper used to treat the incoming poultry litter, followed by trans- port of litter and digestate. Avoided emissions from land spreading of litter are also the most significant process in the acidification impact cat- egory, followed by avoided electricity production. Similarly, production

of the ammonia stripper is amongst the processes with the highest pos- itive value in the acidification category. Incurred fertiliser production and digestate are also sources of SO2 eq, however the overall value for this impact category is negative due to the large value for avoided emis- sions from land spreading. In the eutrophication impact category, there is a net reduction in the baseline scenario. This is largely due to the avoided electricity produc- tion, particularly the avoided coal in the fuel mix. Avoided peat moss production contributes to the net reduction in emissions. Transport, production of the ammonia stripper and incurred fertiliser production are the processes with the largest positive values in this impact cate- gory, however the overall result is negative due to avoided impacts from electricity production.

24: Similar to findings reported by Van Stappen et al. (2016), the results of the baseline scenario show that the impact of the displaced processes are higher than the impact of the plant itself, i.e. the marginal products assumed to be affected are the most important factor in the results. The choice of the marginal product displaced by digestate has a significant impact on the results in the global warming and eutrophication impact categories. When the substitution ratio for digestate to peat is assumed to be 1:0.5 (Scenario 1); emissions savings are 42% lower for global warming and 8% lower for eutrophication. If coir is selected as the
Fig. 4. Results forMonte Carlo simulation of selected impact categories.

marginal product (Scenario 2), emissions savings are similar for global warming compared to the baseline scenario. The environmental savings are higher for the other impact categories largely due to the avoided im- pacts of transporting the coir.

In Scenario 4 an al- ternative fuel mix for electricity generation is included for the year 2030, which excludes coal. In this scenario emissions savings are lower in each of the impact categories compared to the baseline. Nota- bly, there is a net impact for the eutrophication category when coal is excluded. biomethane (scenarios 5, 6 and 7). This shows that the specific timeframe and utilisation pathway for biogas that are selected will in- fluence the results. In the NECP2 scenario, coal is a significant fuel in electricity generation until 2025, where it accounts for 22% of the fuel mix (Government of Ireland, 2018a). After 2025, coal is expected to be excluded from the electricity fuel mix and is constrained. The emissions savings potential of biogas will therefore be reduced in the electricity pathway as there is further decarbonisation of the fuel mix.

In scenario 5, where biogas is upgraded to biomethane, the emis- sions savings are slightly higher than the baseline scenario (21%) when it is assumed biomethane displaces natural gas on a 1:1 basis. However, as discussed in Section 2.5.1, the extent to which one unit of renewable energy displaces one unit of fossil fuel energy is uncertain. In scenarios 6 and 7, the substitution ratios have been varied to examine the impact on the results. Avoided emissions are significantly lower for both scenarios compared to the baseline, however, there is still a net re- duction in the global warming category for both scenarios. The exclu- sion of capital goods (Scenario 3) did not have a significant impact on the results for the chosen impact categories. In Scenario 8, using a higher rate of fugitive emissions from the plant compared to the baseline is sig- nificant in the global warming impact category, with a 21% increase in GWP compared to the baseline.

The net impact arises mainly from incurred fertiliser produc- tion. This is also the case when biogas is assumed to be upgraded to

biomethane (scenarios 5, 6 and 7). This shows that the specific timeframe and utilisation pathway for biogas that are selected will in- fluence the results. In the NECP2 scenario, coal is a significant fuel in electricity generation until 2025, where it accounts for 22% of the fuel mix (Government of Ireland, 2018a). After 2025, coal is expected to be excluded from the electricity fuel mix and is constrained. The emissions savings potential of biogas will therefore be reduced in the electricity pathway as there is further decarbonisation of the fuel mix.

In scenario 5, where biogas is upgraded to biomethane, the emis- sions savings are slightly higher than the baseline scenario (21%) when it is assumed biomethane displaces natural gas on a 1:1 basis. However, as discussed in Section 2.5.1, the extent to which one unit of renewable energy displaces one unit of fossil fuel energy is uncertain. In scenarios 6 and 7, the substitution ratios have been varied to examine the impact on the results. Avoided emissions are significantly lower for both scenarios compared to the baseline, however, there is still a net re- duction in the global warming category for both scenarios. The exclu- sion of capital goods (Scenario 3) did not have a significant impact on the results for the chosen impact categories. In Scenario 8, using a higher rate of fugitive emissions from the plant compared to the baseline is sig- nificant in the global warming impact category, with a 21% increase in GWP compared to the baseline.

29: The three main aspects of uncertainty in LCA are i) quantity (inputs and parameters), ii) model structure, and iii) context (Igos et al., 2019). Quantity uncertainty is present for any data used in the inventory and is accounted for in this assessment using data quality indicators as outlined in Section 2.6. The scores for each parameter are listed in the life cycle inventory (Supplementary information). Reliability of data

and temporal correlation were the indicators that contributed the most to quantity uncertainty, as frequently background data was based on qualified estimates using datasets that were dated from six years or older. The quantity uncertainty is visualised in Fig. 4.

Model structure uncertainty relates to the mismatch between the modelled (mathematical) relationships and the real causal structure of a system, while context uncertainty relates to methodological choices regarding the goal and scope of the study (Igos et al., 2019). This in- cludes the definition of the functional unit, choice of marginal suppliers and indirect consequential effects. Model structure and context uncer- tainties are considered in the scenario analyses. As discussed in Section 2.5.1, the ceteris paribus assumption is a significant source of uncertainty in CLCA. In this study it is assumed that increased use of poultry litter in the system being investigated will not affect broiler pro- duction. However, a feed-in-tariff for the electricity or biomethane pro- duced could be a significant source of revenue for the enterprise if broiler production was co-located with the AD plant. Theoretically this income could be invested to further expand broiler production, for ex- ample. One limitation of CLCA is that it doesn't capture such potential rebound effects.

31: The results of this study indicate that digestion of poultry litter can lead to a net reduction in environmental burdens in the climate change, fine particulate matter formation and terrestrial acidification impact categories. This is in line with previous research which suggests that mono-digestion of manure has lower environmental risks than other feedstocks such as energy crops (Pehme et al., 2017) or those used as animal feed (Styles et al., 2016). In this study, CHP had greater environ- mental savings compared with biogas upgrading in all of the selected impact categories except global warming potential. Tsapekos et al. (2019) found that CHP had a better environmental performance than upgrading of biogas, however, in their assessment the biomethane is intended to be used as a transport fuel, while in this study it is assumed to be used in place of natural gas heating.

As shown in the scenario analyses (Section 3.2), the substitution ratio assumed for the two co-products has a significant impact on the results. The actual changes in consumption and production are deter- mined by the relative supply and demand elasticities with respect to price, as well as the relative strengths of the effects inside and outside the policy region (Plevin et al., 2014). Economic theory provides a means of estimating these effects. Economic-equilibrium models could be used to estimate changes in production and consumption to model non 1:1 substitution ratios in an Irish context. This is particularly rele- vant to inform decision making at a national level. However, the use of nonempirically based price elasticities is an additional source of uncertainty.

While CLCA results are scenario dependent and uncertain, they pro- vide valuable insights about potential environmental effects. In fact, the more comprehensive the consideration of consequential effects, the more uncertain the results (Plevin et al., 2014). No model should be ex- pected to produce a single, definitive quantitative assessment of envi- ronmental outcomes (Plevin et al., 2014). Instead, CLCA is useful for examining alternative scenarios to understand the range of potential environmental outcomes, as opposed to predicting a single most- likely outcome. This should be taken into account when interpreting the results of this study. Once identified, some risks can be more easily avoided rather than precisely quantified. For example, in the case of electricity, the extent to which environmental burdens are reduced de- pends on the grid mix being displaced, while upgrading biogas to biomethane may lead to higher environmental savings if natural gas is always displaced on a 1:1 basis. A decision maker may decide that upgrading to biomethane is preferable in this instance.
The results of this study suggest that AD is a suitable disposal route for poultry litter. In terms of policy support, AD has advantages over

other valorisation technologies in that it is a multifunctional process which facilitates the production of energy as well as a nutrient rich sub- strate. The flexibility of biogas as an energy carrier means that it can be used in various pathways in electricity, heat and transport. The utilisation of biogas in one pathway over another depends on the policy incentives present in a specific region. The results of this study can be used in conjunction with other complimentary modelling approaches such as PE and CGE to determine the optimum pathway in this context.

35: This study uses average data for plant parameters in a single year of operation. As a result, there may be temporal variability which is not captured in the model. For example, the rate of fugitive emissions from a plant may increase over time, however long-term measurements of time variant emissions from biogas plants are lacking (International Energy Agency, 2017). This is considered to some extent in the scenario analysis, where the rate of fugitive emissions is varied in a one-at-a- time sensitivity analysis. Temporal representativeness may be im- proved in future studies if long term data relating to plant operating conditions is available. For data quality, the reliability, completeness, temporal, geographical, and technological correlation of each data point was judged on a scale from 1 to 5. This approach has been criticised for being subjective, however it can be improved with the in- volvement of several experts to confront opinions (Igos et al., 2019). The scores for each data point in this study have been made available with the life cycle inventory.

Uncertainty relating to the selected LCIA methodology, ReCiPe 2016, is not considered in this assessment. Currently characterisation factors are provided without uncertainty (Igos et al., 2019). Selecting the hierarchist perspective for the LCIA in this assessment with its associ- ated characterisation factors is a value-choice, which is largely based on GWP100 as one of the most common climate metrics used in envi- ronmental reporting (Lynch, 2019). However, given recent trends in GHG emissions, other perspectives may be valid. As per the recommen- dation by Lynch (2019), disaggregated emissions of individual gases are available in the underlying data accompanying this article to enable fur- ther analysis with alternative climate metrics.

37: This study used CLCA to investigate the impact of diverting poultry litter from land spreading to AD. The scenario analysis shows that as- sumptions regarding market mechanisms and displaced processes in CLCA can have the greatest impact on the results. The net environmental impact depended mainly on the processes displaced by the use of the two co-products, biogas and digestate. Upgrading biogas to biomethane had the highest GHG emissions savings when assuming 1:1 substitution of natural gas. Environmental savings were reduced if digestate fibre displaced coir instead of peat. The results for the eutrophication impact category varied according to the energy pathway biogas is used in and the fuel mix that is displaced, leading to a net impact in this category in some scenarios. However, for all other impact categories, there is a potential net reduction in environmental burdens for the scenarios con- sidered in this study. CLCA is a useful tool to assess the potential envi- ronmental impacts when diverting feedstocks such as poultry litter to new valorisation pathways in the circular economy. The results indicate that AD is a suitable disposal route for poultry litter which can lead to reduced environmental impacts.
